title: Apollo 16 Astronaut Charlie Duke's Incredible Keynote at the Astro Awards
description:
Brought to you on behalf of the United States Space Force! Thanks so much for helping us get this legend to the 2nd Annual 2025 Astro Awards! @U.S.SpaceForce 

Tickets available for the 3rd annual Astro Awards show on January 17th and 18th in Austin, TX!!! - http://astroawards.live

Enjoy Apollo 16 Lunar Module Pilot, Moon Walker, Test Pilot and absolute legend Charlie Duke's inspiring keynote speech from the 2nd Annual Astro Awards on January 26th, 2025.

--------------------------

Want to support what I do? Consider becoming a Patreon supporter for access to exclusive livestreams, our discord channel! - http://patreon.com/everydayastronaut

Or become a YouTube member for some bonus perks as well! - https://www.youtube.com/channel/UC6uKrU_WqJ1R2HMTY3LIx5Q/join

All music is original! Find it anywhere you listen to music! (Spotify, iTunes, Google Play, Amazon, etc)
full_text: Brigadier General Charles Duke, Apollo [music] 16 astronaut, US Air Force officer, and one of only 12 humans to walk on the moon, is with us today. His story is one of service, courage, and exploration at the highest level. Charlie's journey to space began as a fighter pilot, a graduate of the US Naval Academy, and later an Air Force test pilot. His precision and skill paved the way for his selection as an astronaut for NASA's Apollo program. In 1972, as a lunar module pilot for Apollo 16, Charlie became the youngest person to step foot on the moon at just 36 years old. Now, I want to pause and say, I'm 40. What have I been doing with my life? While on the lunar surface, he conducted experiments, collected samples, and contributed data that continues to shape our understanding of the moon to this day. But to me, one of the most remarkable things about Charlie is the way he connects with people. I'll never forget meeting him almost exactly eight years ago. Tomorrow, it will be exactly 8 years ago to today with my then six-year-old daughter, Millie. Like many kids, she was fascinated by space and thrilled to meet an astronaut. She asked him what he did on the moon, and when he replied, "I collected rocks." Her face fell into a pout and she declared, "That's not fair. you got to go to the moon and I didn't. I feel the exact same way. That moment captured the essence of who Charlie is. Yes, he's a legend of space exploration, but he's also someone whose warmth and humility have inspired generations to dream bigger and aim higher. Even after the Apollo 16 mission, Charlie continued to serve and advance NASA's efforts, leaving a legacy of leadership that paved the way for the future of human space flight. His contributions remind us of what can be achieved through dedication, courage, and a belief in the impossible. As we look ahead to new frontiers, whether it's returning to the moon, reaching for Mars, or safeguarding our assets in orbit, we are fortunate to stand on the shoulders of pioneers like Charlie. So, without further ado, please join me in welcoming a living legend, a man whose achievements have left an indelible mark on the history of exploration, Apollo 16 astronaut, General Charles Duke. [applause] Thank you very much. [applause] Thank you a lot. Thank you for great introduction. Thank you so much. Thank you. [cheering] Thank you very much. Thank you. Thanks so much. Please be seated. It's a great uh pleasure being here. I want to thank the space US Space Force for uh sponsoring uh uh this event and our trip. And so, uh, getting to meet, uh, those who are now recruiting for the, uh, you, uh, Air Force for the Space Force. It's, uh, great meeting you. Uh, they worked at the recruiting service at Randolph and where I worked my last, uh, eight years in the Air Force Reserve. So, it's, uh, good to I was recruiting 18 year olds back in those days. Uh it was uh in the early uh uh let's see or uh '7s I guess before that. My career is just going uh in my mind now trying to get the years straight. But uh anyway uh it's good to be here. I I want to talk about Apollo uh and how it got started uh and a little bit about uh my career. Uh I tell all a lot of young people I said just keep your antennas up because you never know where your career is going to take you. Uh and uh sensitive to urges uh and uh at the Naval Academy I had this urge to I just loved airplanes and they gave me some rides in airplanes. So uh uh I decided to back then you could go in the air force and uh from the naval academy and so I went to the NA I went into the air force and in uh October of 19 uh uh I just soloed when Sputnik went up the beginning of the space age uh really got our attention uh in the USN uh uh with the first satellite in in in orbit. it we u uh were pressing on with our space program but it was not very successful in those early days. Uh anyway, I graduated from flight school uh uh flight training and was assigned to Germany 1957. No, 19 59. And in the spring of 1959, I went over to Germany. They picked the first group of astronauts. NASA had been formed. So, the space race was on, if you will. And uh so I uh I was just loving my job over there. And it was a tremendous flying uh uh at Rammstein Air Base in uh in Germany. So, uh, the while I was on the way over, I said I I think I mentioned the first group of astronauts was selected, and I didn't even have a a an idea about being an astronaut, but I I wanted to advance my career. And and a year and a half later, uh, we in 1961, Yuri Gagarin was the first human being in space. the Russians beat us and then Allan Shepard was the next and uh then after that uh there was a commitment uh to by President Kennedy to the go to the moon on Apollo program. Well later on I found out that uh Wernner Brawn had written a letter to then president uh I have a copy of that letter and in this letter he said Mr. president, we can't beat him here. We can't beat him there, but we can beat him to the moon. And so, I think based on that letter, Kennedy accepted his advice and committed us uh to go to the moon. Uh and we didn't know how we're going to do it. Uh and uh I followed the development and there was a big debate about whether we uh rendevued in Earth orbit or rendevued at the moon. Well, it was decided that uh we would take up everything in one rocket, the lunar module, the command module, all that you needed to get into uh get to the moon. So they the Saturn 5 was developed by Marshall Space Flight Center and uh that uh was the vehicle that every Apollo mission that went to the moon was riding on top of. It was 363 feet tall. It was weighed 6 12 million pounds. It had 7 12 million pounds of thrust in the first stage. And uh you're sitting up on top of that thing. And uh and what you really and windows are covered over. You can't see outside and you're just listening to the count. And what you really were thinking about keep counting. Keep counting. I don't want to abort. and you had a 4hour window to get off and we had trained due to uh some problems. We had delayed two years and I'd been training for two years with John Young. We were ready to go. And so we were uh laying there and uh John was on the left side, commander, I was on the right side and uh and just keep counting, keep counting. And then t-minus 8 seconds, they lit the engines and we started to vibrate from side to side. No pogo, but from side to side. And I didn't remember anybody telling me it was supposed to shake this hard. So, uh, I got a little nervous and, uh, the, uh, the, uh, and but John was very cool. It was his second flight on the Saturn, uh, five and he said, "We're go, Houston." Houston said, "We're go." And so we went. It took 8 seconds to clear the tower. And then and then the next 2 minutes and 40 seconds or whatever it was, uh, we accelerated out to use the first stage. And in and in that time of the first stage burn and we got to 35 mi I think it was altitude and uh we'd burned up 4 million pounds of fuel. Over half the weight of the vehicle was gone in the first couple of minutes and the shaking was really and we were accelerating four and a half times gravity and everybody told us about we they called it the train wreck when they shut down the engines for you went from four and a half gs to zero instantly. So you stiff armed the spa the instrument panel to get even though you were strapped in tight. Anyway, after that, uh, shut down start of the second stage, boost protective cover came off and we could see outside for the first time. And we were going in heads down, we call it. And I looked out the window, which was right up here, and I could see the deep blue of the Atlantic and fading into the light blue of the atmosphere into the blackness of space. And all that was in this little window. It was really spectacular. We orbited the earth one and a half times over Australia. Uh we uh reignited the uh uh third stage of the Saturn and accelerated to escape velocity. Uh and we were on a 26 mile uh I mean a 26 degree uh orbit. So we got 26 north to 26,000. We fired over Australia and we started back down to the south. Later on, uh, we were out of orbit and, uh, so, uh, Mattingley had to, we had to retrieve the lunar module. And so he did that, uh, separated. We turned around and as I was, uh, just floating there, uh, watching these guys maneuver into my window on the right side, floated the Earth. It's about 20,000 miles away. And you could see the whole circle of the earth in the Arctic Circle down across Canada, US, Mexico, uh, northern part of South America. And it was just this jewel just suspended in the blackness of space and which we had seen of course from Apollo pictures that had been taken, but this view of there it was just suspended there and you couldn't see any evidence of civilization. We could see the Rocky Mountains, the southwestern United States, the Gulf of Mexico, uh the uh Gulf of California and that all was very visible and but [clears throat] it didn't you couldn't see any evidence of people. There was no cities b visible and uh it was uh we were on our way and it was uh and you were just so excited about that. I had a little feeling of I was going to get uh air sick, but uh that passed in about a couple hours and I didn't didn't have to uh use the bag uh that [clears throat] several of us had in the past. Uh anyway, we took three Apollo took three days to get to the moon, 72 hours. And we got to the moon and um uh the u uh in went into orbit and [clears throat] a day later we were on the back side of the moon and we were one hour before we were to land on the moon. We all powered up in the lunar module. We'd separated and Mattingly had to change his orbit from a 60-mi uh by 8 mile to a 60-mi circular orbit. And he had to light the main engine. And when he tried to do that, there was something wrong with the control system. And John said, "Don't burn." When John said that, that we weren't going to land in the next hour. And you can imagine uh oh god I won't use the language here but that we had on board but it was uh man you know an hour before and we're going to abort this mission. Fortunately mission control saved the day as they did in Apollo. Mission control were the heroes of Apollo. They and every flight they overcame some problem that was going to abort the mission and uh or either on Apollo 13 for instance they came up with all of the stuff that got them back safely. So I look at mission control as the heroes of Apollo and their their expertise and all of the flight controllers. So, uh, [clears throat] we orbit, we came around from the backside and they said, "Well, stand by." Uh, we're looking at it and it took two rails, two orbits, and on the the second orbit around, um, they said, "We we know what's wrong. We can't fix it, but here's a workaround, and you're go for landing on the next rail." Yahoo! We, you know, and and the flight controller made that decision. I mean there were big NASA e experts in that room the NASA hierarchy but they know make those decisions the flight crew does I mean the flight director made that decision named Jerry Griffin he said your go on the next rev and you for your landing and you you cleared the burn. So we started our descent and uh I'm going to start this uh let's see here. Well, this is some training stuff. That's me on the right, John Young. We had That's the uh in New Mexico, we had a rover. We were the second crew to have a rover. Uh and uh we had this one that worked downtown down on Earth. And John's driving and I'm the navigator on the left side up there. Uh [clears throat] and this is our crew. Uh I'm on the right there. Uh 36. John was the commander was his fourth mission, a second on Apollo and TK Mattingley. I'm the only one left alive uh of that crew. Some of the Apollo crews uh all of them are dead. Uh and uh we uh have only four of us moon walkers left alive. Uh I'm the youngest and I'm 89. And so somebody out there please uh let's go to the moon again. So [cheering] I thank you. [applause] I want to I want to pass that baton to the next moon walker before uh I go to glory. So, uh, uh, anyway, uh, Mattingley's passed on, John's passed on, and so I'm, uh, here, uh, as a one of four survivor surviving moon walkers. This was the configuration that we went to the moon. Uh, we had, uh, the lunar module. We had to dock uh, and extract it from the third stage of the Saturn. Uh we we rode in the uh in the uh command module which is on the left our right lunar module was there and then uh we had a we had a looked like a leak when we got joined up and we powered up the lunar module to see make sure that we were okay and it was. And so we closed back up again and went uh went to the moon in the command module. And uh uh this is the uh the well yeah this is the view. This is the Earth that we really saw. And uh it was blue and white and brown. All the land from space looks brown. We're This is now our Earth rise. So we we're in orbit. Uh this is at this point we weren't going to land. Uh and uh so uh here is the command module a couple miles in front of us. The earth is up here and we're uh taking this picture over the rough lunar lunar terrain. the they had to get it fixed in a hurry because the moon was rotating out from under us and we had three revolutions that uh and if it got past we delayed three revolutions we weren't going to be able to make it back to the uh uh to the landing site. Uh so uh we were I can't say praying at that point but we were really hoping that mission control would come up with a right uh solution and they did and so uh we uh uh uh went to uh uh we we started our our landing. This is a a picture of the just the front face of the moon. It shows where all of the Apollo landing uh sites were. Uh uh 11 was the first, of course. Here we are in the mountains of the moon called the Daycart Highlands. And from Apollo 11 to Apollo 16, there was 8,000 ft difference in elevation. So, we landed up in the uh in the in the mountains. The farthest uh was 12 was the farther's west. Uh the farther's north was 15 and the farther's east 17's not on here, but it's right up in here. And so of the five missions to the moon, we covered sort of the front face. Uh uh Artemis that's coming up uh their landing zone is uh down here on the south pole. And uh the sun is go not going to get ever above two degrees uh there. And so the if you look at the photos that have been taken down there, it's really rough and is also really dark in the shadows. So uh they've got a challenging uh landing site in my opinion. so I've discussed there's that direct landing site and you can see uh if if you look at the uh uh uh lunar surveyor uh ele uh the lunar surveyor uh spacecraft they taken pictures of all the Apollo landing sites and you can see in our landing site you can see uh the car you can see the descent stage you can see the experiments package and all of the the the basically the tracks, not individual footprints, but the tracks. And um so the evidence is overwhelming that we landed on the moon and thank you. So I told uh on TV this 15 20 years ago I guess it was when this big hoax deal came about and I said Katie Coric I said why did Katie if we faked it why did we fake it nine times and uh you know we had six landings and we went to the moon time times if you're going to fake it do it once and shut up. uh but 400,000 people and not one of them has ever called it a lie that worked on that program. And so uh the as I said uh I don't know whether he sent the other guys but I landed on the moon. Uh this is uh uh my words when we landed uh and John did a great job. he landed it and I've just talked him down uh looking out the right window occasionally, make sure he's not landing in a big crater on the right or in a on a big rock. Uh and uh so he picked out uh he had to redesate a couple of times and then picked out a spot and when we touched down we were dead level. So he made a a a great decision. We looked out of his window and uh as we look to the uh south down here uh this is south uh and uh yeah south and uh this is Stone Mountain and uh on a second excursion we drove the car up into this area right here. spectacular view from up there. And uh the shadows always west, the sun was always behind us. Uh and it was always daylight on all of Apollo. The space suits would not take uh ab uh darkness on the on the moon. So we were always on the moon during the daylight, early morning of the moon day. And the shadows were very long and there and we used uh since there's no magnetic field up on the moon, we used the shadow is direction and west the shadow was west uh and we had a little gyro on the car and we rolled it up till 270 was at the top and uh that was the direction and John I navigated across the moon and John drove uh and uh it was a bumpy ride. Uh and uh but uh he did a a did a great job. Uh well, let's see here now. Oh, here we go. Okay, that's uh John Young um uh giving me a a big salute uh on uh jumping off the moon. If you notice, his shadows not touching his feet. So, uh he's about two feet off the moon. I said, "John, give me a big Navy salute." So he came out, jumped and gave me a big salute like that. And uh and uh so uh I got that picture as he was in the air. Uh and um a lot of people use this picture. Look at the flag. It's waving and there's no atmosphere on the moon. And uh and I mean it's those shoes, but they just put him in there and he's not even on the uh on the ground. And uh I mean that crazy. Well, so anyway, the the the the uh oops, let me back up here. Uh the um the flag is it's got a curtain rod in it holding it out. And it was vacuum packed for six months and I couldn't get the wrinkle out of it. And uh so uh I just planted it in there. Well, 72 hours later as we were leaving, you take I took a picture and it was the same wrinkle. So uh anyway, uh let's go on here now. And uh we had a lot of experiments we put put out. Uh each flight had a a Apollo lunar surface experiments package. There were five experiments. Uh, and here they're listed over here. And, uh, we had, uh, three EVAs. Uh, we had, um, uh, the longest was almost eight hours. Uh, we had a telescope that we put up for the, uh, uh, for the, uh, uh, for the US Naval Observatory, this thing here. Uh, and John deployed that. And uh so we were taking pictures of uh the uh in in invisible to us invisible light. Uh here's a car practicing on the rover. Uh this is what it uh it looks like. Uh see where's the clicker go? Anyway, it's folded up. This car had to be folded up to and bolted to the outside of the spacecraft. And so the the chassis came in three three spots, three spu uh stages. So the place to store it on the car on the lunar module was 5x5 and the car was 5x10. So they made it 5x5 by the making the chassis in three spots and folding it up so that all the wheels put together. And then we had to deploy it uh once we got uh uh once we got on the moon. And John John pulled on one side with a cable. I pulled on the other side. We jacked it out and it got out about 45 degrees and boom, it just popped open. And uh we lowered it down to the moon and uh and we un undid the front part and then John we got on one side and I got on one and we just walked off with it. And the uh car weighed uh 80 lbs up on the moon. And uh uh and in our in my situation here uh uh down on the down here on the earth, I weighed 363 lbs. Up on the moon, 60 lb. And uh John, I mean, you could really bounce a lot, but it was hard for me to hold my balance. So, uh I didn't uh do much uh jumping. Uh John had a a great balance. And uh so he's driving and I'm taking a it's a Lunar Grand Prix and I don't have a movie of it, but it's online on YouTube. It's about five minutes and it shows the car underway bouncing like crazy uh on the lunar surface. Uh at this point right here, uh the fenders were okay, but I pulled this accidentally pulled this part of the fender off on the right rear and the and the dust was pouring down on top of us. So uh uh it was a a big mistake not fixing that fender, but uh uh we did not. Uh, and uh, uh, here's John. John had the the commander had the red stripes and Luna module pilot didn't have any stripes. So there's the experiments package that we put out. Uh, and uh, John's collecting uh, some sample there. We collected 300 uh, let's see uh, 260 pounds I think. uh 98 kilos, whatever that turns out to be. And uh uh and uh we usually work together. You can see the footprints up on the moon. Uh we we never sank in more than just an inch or two, but when you started to shovel, you could shovel all the way down to you couldn't shovel anymore, and it was still loose soil. Uh then I drilled a a a 10-ft pole uh 10- foot deep hole and we jacked it back out and it was still unconsolidated uh 10 ft down at least in this landing spot. But uh you never you never sank in more than just a a couple of millimeters, a couple of centimeters rather. And you can see the the footprints here. They're still there. Uh and uh and so uh we uh ended up uh as I said collecting a lot of special samples uh as we were uh on the moon. We had three EVAs uh out sat us outside and uh the commander uh you ever wonder why Neil Armstrong was the first out? Well, to get out there was I'm standing on the right side. The commander stands on the left side and and to get out you had to get on your hands and knees backwards and go out the hatch which was between us and when you open the hatch you open the hatch and the and the hatch came this way. So the lunar module pilot's trapped behind the hatch. First guy outs the commander. So, uh, then he gets out and then I close the hatch or close the door a little bit and then open up and then I turn around and rear end first and you go out uh that way. And uh it was hard to get through the hatch uh because you of the backpack and everything you had to make sure you wiggle your way around to get get through. But anyway, we trained I can't tell you how many times we trained that down here as that exercise down here on Earth. Uh one of the big craters uh uh that we visited uh on the end, it was 300 uh this crater actually was about 500 yards across and about a 100 yards deep. And if you got right close so you could see down in it and it sloughed off and you fell in, there was no rescue, no rescue. And so we uh ended up uh we ended up uh um staying quite a bit away from these uh uh uh these uh uh big craters. Uh, one of the most uh, moving moments of a mission for me was right at the end. Uh, our boys were five and seven at the time. Uh, and we took a a picture and of the family in the back in the backyard and uh, of the four of us. And I asked Dick Sllayton, "Can I take this picture of the moon?" He said, "Sure." And I said, "I'd like to leave it on me." And he says, "Sure." So, uh, I had it in my pocket of my suit and I pulled it out and I dropped it and it actually came it just there was no air to make it wet. It just when I dropped it, it just landed face up. And so I got a picture of the picture and uh, the temperature on the surface of the moon at this point, average surface was about 210° Fahrenheit. So, it was getting real hot on the surface and you can start to see I just dropped this picture, but uh uh the uh you can see the uh it's beginning to shrivel up around the the corners already. It's still there, but it's just a big black uh spot now after 50 something years. And um so uh all of the equipment we left is still there. If you want a$ eight million dollar car with a dead battery, I can tell you where to go. And uh it's uh it's all the three cars are still there. And uh but we're not going to be landing anywhere near uh Apollo with uh Artemis. Uh this is just an idea of what the surface looked like and where we went. Uh we named all the craters uh and uh they weren't official but we named them so that mission control could know where we were and uh the science team and uh but uh they just were numbered as a result of uh our uh stuff. Uh you can see the descent stage here. Uh here's the descent stage right here. uh and that there's you can see the car and all the uh the um the experiments package it was out here and there's footprints and all of those things. Uh so uh again the evidence is overwhelming uh uh that we made it uh and uh it says I got nine m on schedule or way over uh I got the sign says nine minutes to go. So, uh uh I wanted to to uh uh give a chance to ask some questions. Uh our return was uh uneventful. Uh we hit uh uh Apollo uh it didn't have wings, but it had lift because the center of gravity was offset from the center of the heat shield. So you came in and in and you re-entry with lift vector up and and when you hit the atmosphere, it wanted to bounce out like skipping a rock across the water. And if you allowed that to happen, you were in deep trouble. In fact, you were dead. You you couldn't get back. And uh so as mission control said gave us at a time and so at a certain time as you were digging in we hit the atmosphere like 25,000 miles an hour and uh maybe a little faster. And as it started back out it rolled over the autopilot just pulled us back in. It was like airplane maneuver of split S if you p if you're a pilot. And uh we came back in and then began to maneuver to hit the landing spot in the South Pacific. Uh and uh it all uh worked uh uh tremendously well. Uh let's go see here. Uh there we go. Uh off we go. And uh what a ride. I was saying what a ride. It was really a sporty ride. And uh the reaction control diff was moving it to hold its trajectory. And um this was taken by uh the camera that we'd left on the on the moon uh on the rover. Uh let's see. I think that's about it. Well, uh I'm going to uh give a chance to ask some questions now. We we uh we survived the landing of course uh we uh hit in the South Pacific. The carrier was on station and u so I'm the windows. I'm looking out the window and I see this helicopter fly by and Maddingley's on the left side over there and uh we're on three parachutes coming down and and I see the helicopter and I'm up on my elbows looking out the window and Maddenley's counting down, you know, 1,900 800 and and I said, "When he gets to 100, I'm going to put my head back and brace myself." when we he called 100, we hit the water and and my head went back like that. And and my first job was after we hit the water was to push a circuit breaker in off my right knee so he could jet us in the parachutes. Well, I mean, I'm just sort of I'm not unconscious, but I'm not too there at first. And so, it finally took me a 30 seconds or more to push the the breaker in. And by this time the parachutes had pulled us over and we were inverted. And Apollo will float would float right side up or upside down. And it was stable in each position. So we had some big balloons in the in the um uh in the um the top of the spacecraft which is now in the water. And so uh I got the circuit breaker in Mattingly Jettison in the shoots and then he punched the turn on the switch. just started blowing up these big uh balloons and in about five minutes it flipped us back over and we were right side up and uh so we're home free and the Navy was there and picked us up and uh we got back to Tyonderoga and uh was uh mission complete. Uh so uh how about some question? I can't see very well out there. So, maybe we got uh >> And actually, Charlie, I'm going to I'm going to take this opportunity because no offense, guys, but uh I have some great questions for this man here. So, as a lot of you know, [applause] I'm I'm taking this opportunity. As a lot of you know, I've been working on a deep dive video for two It's uh for 8 months. It's 2 and a half hours long. And I've got some questions that I would absolutely love if you could answer for me. >> Okay. Number one, why didn't you make the ladder any longer? Because you guys had to jump down the ladder and it the bottom rung was like, >> well, it was about the idea was if you landed hard uh you this the the landing gear would crush. Uh and uh so they would it had honey chrome crushable in all of the legs and if you hit hard they would stroke and it would leave it could leave uh if the ladder was uh into the dirt it would be a problem. So uh they they stopped the ladder about 2 feet 3 feet above uh above the surface is the reason. >> Yeah. Was it a challenge at all then to get up and down that last little bit of ladder or was that >> It was easy getting off. Uh it was uh we >> getting back in is maybe the more important part. >> Yeah, we practice though in the in the vomit comet. Uh we did uh a lot of practice. They'd have a ladder in there and you you'd practice one6 jumping up and uh and so yes, you we'd practice all of this >> and uh it wasn't just the first time you did it and in one sixth gravity. So u uh it wasn't any problem. You you jump and grab hold and uh one foot was going to hit the rungs on the ladder. So uh uh we didn't have any problem. The only trouble was if you jumped John could do it. He he'd have a rock box in one hand and he'd jump and and hit the ladder and and and climb up with this rock box in his hand. I couldn't do that. I just jumped and and uh held on and uh and made it. [laughter] Can you imagine your life is now uh relying on you being able to jump three feet on the moon and grab onto a ladder? I mean, pretty spectacular. Well, I I uh I hadn't practiced that except in the lunar in the vomit comet and uh in 16 gravity uh parabas uh and uh so uh when uh we uh decided uh that before we were to shut get back in the last time we were going to do uh the moon Olympics and uh you You know, Apollo 15 had done the hammer feather trick and uh Alan Shepard had hit the golf ball and so we were going to do the moon Olympics and the Olympics were in Munich that year and so we're going to do the high jump and the Roy jump. So, we started bouncing and I'm behind the rover and I and I when I jumped I straightened up and my center of gravity went backwards and over I went and that was really scary and I think that was probably the only time I had fear in the whole mission. But fear is not bad emotion if you don't panic. And so roll right and I rolled to the right and broke my fall and on my back and man heart was pounding like that. But I'm I real I'm still alive. So uh John comes over and and looks down and says that wasn't very smart, Charlie. So uh I said, "Help me up, John." So I you you could hear the pumps running and the pressure was real good and the all the uh all the the uh in the stuff all the instruments said I'm okay. So I begin to calm down. Then I looked up the and the TV camera was pointed right at me and uh and they'd seen this stupid stunt and uh they said, "No more moon Olympics, guys. Get back inside." So I think we set the high jump record, but I'm not sure.
